Lu Mao (Zizhang)
陸瑁 (子璋)
Lived ADca.190–239

Lu Mao, styledZizhang, was the younger brother ofAssisting Chancellor[Lu] Xun.
When he was young, he was fond of studying and revered bonds.Chen RongofChen state[陳國],Puyang YiofChenliu[陳留],Jiang ZuanofPei commandery[沛郡], andYuan DiofGuangling[廣陵] were all poor and destitute, but had ambition. They went to[Lu] Maoto travel and lodge together,Aand[Lu] Maodivided even the small things he had and shared sweetness1with them. They would collectively experience being wealthy and being frugal.Xu Yuanof the same commandery resided inKuaiji[會稽] and never knew him well, but before he died, his parting letter entrusted him with his orphaned and helpless [sons].[Lu] Maowould erect a tomb for him and support his sons. Also, the cousin of his father,[Lu] Ji, died young, and had two sons and one daughter. All of them were several years old, and when they returned [to their hometown],[Lu] Maoreceived them and took care of them. When they grew older, theydeparted.•••
Here, “shared sweetness” [分甘] is a metaphor for close bonds.↩
The grandson of[Yuan] Diwas[Yuan] Ye, who wrote theChronicles of the Astute Emperor, and it stated that[Yuan] DiandZhang Hong, among others, crossed theYangzi River[江]. The father of[Yuan] Diwas[Yuan] Sui, who served as abureaucratunder theGrand Tutor. WhenZhang Chaowas suppressingDong Zhuo, he had[Yuan] Suimanage affairs inGuangling[廣陵].
The province and commandery recruited and nominated him, but he did not accept them all. At the time,Secretariat MinisterJi Yanflaunted his wisdom of what was good and what was bad, and in selecting and judging [officials of the] Three Offices,2he would disseminate hidden flaws of the people more often than not, then display their punishments.[Lu] Maowrote a letter to him,stating:•••
This refers to the officials who served in the Office of the Five Ranks [五官署], Office of the Right [右署], and Office of the Left [左署], and generally refers to high-ranking officials. Ji Mao would review and judge them for their performance, and often times did he demote them.↩
“The sage who admires the capable, pities the foolish, forgets about faults, and records achievements is able to achieve respectable moral influence. This is all the more so when the royal enterprise has been founded in recent times and is aiming to bring all under the great unification. Hence, it is this time to shirk shortcomings and to instead employ and appoint [people], as did theSupreme[Emperor] ofHan. If we engender the virtuous and the sinful to be of different streams,3and if we value theRu[汝] andYing[穎] evaluation of the first monthly dawn,4then it would indeed be possible to refine customs and expound education. However, I fear this would not be feasible to implement. We ought to model ourselves after the universal love ofZhongniof ancient times5and follow the liberal salvation of Guo Tai [郭泰] of more recent times6so that in these times, we can improve the great moralway.”•••
Essentially, separating good and evil.↩
The famousXu ShaoandXu JingofRunanandYingchuanappraised notable people, typically on the first day of a month.↩
Zhongnihad once said, “Love the masses universally, and bond closely with the benevolent.” [泛愛眾，而親仁]↩
The idea that a person should help everyone.↩
[Ji] Yanwould not act on this, and he eventually brought about his downfall.
In the first year of theera [232], the chariot of anExcellencywas used to summon[Lu] Mao. He was conferred to serve asGentleman ConsultantandSecretariat Minister of the Nomination Bureau.Sun Quanwas resentful over the deceit and duplicity ofGongsun Yuan, and he wanted to personally conduct an expedition against him.[Lu] Maopetitioned to protest, stating:
“This minister of yours has heard that when a sage king controlled distant barbarians, it would be through reins and nothing more;7they would often not ensure that they were under possession. Thus, when the ancients administered such lands, they described them as barren domains,8which is to say that they were obscure and inconstant, unable to be ensured. Presently, the eastern barbarian[Gongsun] Yuanis but a petty brute who hides in a coastal region. Though he may seem to have the face of a human, he is no different than a savage beast. The reason why the empire granted the goods and treasures they did not cherish from afar was not because they admired his virtue and righteousness, but truly because they wanted to entice and manipulate him in order to gain his horses.[Gongsun] Yuanis arrogant and cunning, and he relied on his far distance to betray your commands. Such is the wont of a wasteland barbarian; how can it necessitate your deep censure? The various emperors ofHanalso once focused on conducting business with foreign barbarians. They sent envoys on horseback to distribute goods such that they filled the western frontier. Even though there was respect and compliance [by the barbarians] from time to time, the instances in which envoys were killed and their wealth and goods were all taken could not be calculated. Now that the furious rage ofYour Majestycannot be contained, you want to go beyond the vast ocean and personally trample on his lands. Amidst the humble discourse of the ministers, I was feeling uneasy. Why? Theenemy of the northborders our empire and their lands are adjacent to ours. If there is a rift, they will take the opportunity and come. The reason we went across the ocean to ask for horses and deigned ourselves to[Gongsun] Yuanwas so that we could resolve troubles happening before our eyes and remove problems festering within ourselves. For us to instead abandon the fundamental to chase after the trivial and to ignore what is closer to us to pursue what is further to us, changing our plans out of fury and mobilizing the army out of impetuousness is exactly what ourconniving foedesires to hear about, and it would not be the best plan forGreatWu. Moreover, a strategy in warfare is to weaken the target through the amount of work they committed and to await the target through the amount of energy they committed; the differences between our gains and our losses will be many upon realization. What is more, the roads from the banks ofTa[沓] to[Gongsun] Yuanis still quite far. Now if we arrive at their shores, we would have to divide our army into three, having the strongest advance forth to conquer, the second [strongest] guard the boats, and the least [strongest] transport provisions. Even though there would be many people on the journey, it will be difficult to utilize them all. In addition, the provisions would have to be carried on our backs while walking deep into [their territory]. There are many horses in the lands of that traitor, and they can intercept us unpredictably. If[Gongsun] Yuanis beguiling us and he has not severed [ties] with thenorthyet, then on the day we mobilize the army, they would help each other as lips to teeth, but if he is alone and he has nothing he would rely upon, then he would escape afar out of fear. Either way, it would be difficult to destroy him in a hurry. Should Heaven cause our retribution to stall in the northern lands, that opportunity would be seized by mountain brigands to rebel. I fear this is not a plan for us to be absolutely secure in the longterm.”•••
Past kingdoms would grant titles to “barbarians”, enter marriage alliances with them, and trade with them to ensure that they would be influenced. This is how they used “reins” to control them.↩
Here, “barren domains” [荒服] was a term used to describe lands inhabited by “barbarians” that were thousands of miles away from the capital.↩
[Sun] Quandid not accept this.[Lu] Maopetitioned again, stating:
“Weaponry and armor was verily used by past dynasties to exterminate violent rebellions and demonstrate might to theFour Barbarians, but such expeditions all occurred at a time where vile traitors were quelled and the realm was without any problems, being discussed among many other discussions in a calm court. If there was boiling chaos in the central plains and the mutual entanglement of theNine Provinces[九域], they would hurriedly deepen their roots and solidify their foundation, cherishing their strength and saving their finances while striving hard to enhance themselves in order to await any flaws of their bordering enemies. Never during such a time did they abandon what was closer to them to pursue what was further to them, lest they exhausted their military. When Commandant [Zhao] Tuo [趙佗] rebelled and illegitimately declared himselfEmperor, the world was in peace and stability, the common people were prospering and thriving, and the amount of armor and food can be said to have been plenty. Nevertheless, theCivil[Emperor] ofHanthought that a distant expedition would not be feasible and the mustering of military men too much, so he only made explanations to him.9Presently, thevicious menacehas not been exterminated yet and there are still alarms at the borders such that supposing if we had troubles like those of Chiyou [蚩尤] and Guifang [鬼方],10then in judging what is pressing, it would not yet be appropriate to regard[Gongsun] Yuanas a priority. I hope thatYour Majestywill mitigate your mightiness and pause your plans, and instead give some temporary respite to the whole army11and concentrate on plotting for your future plans. This would be great blessing to therealm.”•••
Zhao Tuo was a military officer who took control of Nanyue and declared himself emperor following worsening ties between him and theHan dynasty. However, when theCivilEmperorofHanappeased him by acting diplomatically rather than aggressively and treated his family outside of Nanyue with great respect, Zhao Tuo agreed to be a nominal vassal again and renounced his claim as emperor outside of Nanyue.↩
Chiyou was said to be a vassal of Shennong in at least one account and the Guifang people were said to be vassals of theShang dynasty. Both of them launched largescale rebellions against their masters that lasted for years. How much of this is legend and history is unknown.↩
The literal term for “whole army” is “six armies” [六師], referring to the six armies that were directly under royal control during theZhou dynasty. However, later on, it became a general term used to refer to the imperial army.↩
When[Sun] Quanread the letter of[Lu] Mao, he admired that the sentiment of the words was candid and sincere. He thus did not travel.
Initially,Wenren Minof the same commandery as[Lu] Maowas given special treatment in the capital, better than that ofZong Xiu. Only[Lu] Maobelieved this was not so, and later, it was indeed as he said.
In the second year of theera [239],[Lu] Maodied. His son[Lu] Xi, also was experienced at writing books and he was good at appraising people. During the time of Sun Hao, he served asSecretariat Minister of the Nomination Bureau.B
TheRecords of Wustates:[Lu] Xi[陸喜], styled Wenzhong [文仲], was the second son of[Lu] Mao. He enteredJinto serve asCavalier Regular AttendantThe grandson of[Lu] Maowas [Lu] Ye [陸曄], styled Shiguang [士光]. He attained the rank ofGeneral of Chariots and CavalryandDignitary with Respects Akin to the Three Excellencies. The younger brother of [Lu] Ye was [Lu] Wan [陸玩], styled Shiyao [士瑤].
TheChronicles of Jinacclaims [Lu] Wan of being generous and elegant in talent and capability. He reachedExcellency of Worksin rank and was posthumously madeGrand Commandant.
ㅤ
